justification: All pass criteria met: (1) At least 3 bounded contexts identified — Route Planning/Optimization, Fleet/Driver Management, Delivery Lifecycle/Management, and Customer Notifications are all named in the business_context and bounded_context fields. (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified — the bounded_context field states 'Route Planning consumes events from adjacent contexts' and names Fleet Management (vehicle class), Delivery Management (lifecycle states), and Customer Notification (proximity alerts) as crossing boundaries. (3) Correct invariant classification — vehicle class constraint BR-2 is invariant:true, stop duration BR-7 is invariant:false, notification window BR-6 is invariant:false. (4) 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well above the 4 minimum. (5) Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated in BR-3 (new delivery), BR-4 (failed delivery), BR-5 (driver deviation).

observations.text: Comprehensive bounded context identification with 4 contexts named and their relationships described
10 well-structured acceptance criteria covering core flows and edge cases like empty zones and contradictory notifications
Correct invariant classification across all rules — vehicle class (true), zone constraint (true), recalculation triggers (false), stop duration (false), notification window (false)
Rich edge case analysis with 10 scenarios including concurrent recalculation, multi-driver partitioning, and mid-route config changes
Cross-context event-driven nature of route recalculation explicitly called out as consuming events from Delivery Lifecycle

Route recalculation triggers BR-3/4/5 marked invariant:false — the rubric expected invariant:true since the system must always recalculate on these events. The justification given (business could batch or defer) is reasonable but diverges from expected classification
Cross-context dependencies could be more formally structured — mentioned in prose within bounded_context field rather than as explicit integration point entries for MJ

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Optimization, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification). (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified — multi-driver zone coordination escalation, vehicle class as cross-context constraint between Fleet and Delivery, recalculation triggers spanning Delivery→Route contexts. (3) Vehicle class rule (BR-6) correctly marked invariant:true; stop time (BR-7) correctly marked invariant:false; notification window (BR-5) correctly marked invariant:false. (4) 10 acceptance criteria provided, well above the 4 minimum. (5) Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated: new delivery added, delivery marked failed, driver manual deviation.

observations.text: Four well-defined bounded contexts with clear ownership boundaries and explicit statement that contexts must communicate via domain events
10 detailed Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering core flows and edge cases like zero deliveries, multi-driver zones, and vehicle class filtering
Rich edge case analysis (10 scenarios) covering race conditions in recalculation, notification idempotency, and unassignable deliveries
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations — optimization objective ambiguity, deviation detection, multi-driver coordination, and recalculation throttling
Vehicle class constraint (BR-6) correctly classified as invariant:true with strong safety/regulatory justification

Route recalculation triggers (BR-2, BR-3, BR-4) marked invariant:false — the rubric expects invariant:true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's justification (policy could change to batch/defer) is defensible but diverges from the expected classification

Cross-context dependency between Fleet Management (vehicle class) and Delivery Management explicitly surfaced in AC-6 and AC-10, and the multi-driver coordination escalation highlights the Route↔Delivery boundary
